ch1-scene0: A narrow, dimly-lit attic corridor opens to reveal a young man descending a cramped, peeling staircase. The oppressive heat seeps through dusty windows, casting harsh amber light over battered, paint-flecked walls. The young man, tall, gaunt, with unruly dark hair and threadbare, out-of-fashion clothing, moves warily to avoid the open kitchen door below, behind which a stern landlady can be glimpsed among shadows. The atmosphere is tense and claustrophobic, embodying St. Petersburg in the mid-19th century.
ch1-scene1: A bustling, grimy St. Petersburg street in the sweltering summer twilight, lined with crooked buildings and scaffolding. Workers pass by in linen shirts and caps, while disheveled townsfolk mingle with drunken figures. Raskolnikov, in his shabby coat and battered hat, stands amidst the dust and confusion, his tall, thin frame lost and anxious in the chaos. A heavy waggon pulled by a dray horse passes by, carrying a raucous drunk, while the stench of pot-houses and the close air flattens the scene.
ch1-scene2: At the grimy base of a massive tenement building typical of 19th-century St. Petersburg, Raskolnikov slips unnoticed through a shadowy side door. The back staircase is dark, narrow, and heavily worn, lit by faint daylight filtering in from a landing. Porters in work clothes maneuver heavy furniture down the passage, while Raskolnikov, tense and cautious, sneaks past them. The atmosphere is close and anonymous, the corridors echoing with the sounds of labor and movement from the occupants.
ch1-scene3: A cramped, meticulously tidy 19th-century Petersburg flat, washed in the golden, slanted light of the setting sun. Flowered muslin curtains, scrubbed yellow floors, and a few austere pieces of yellow-wood furniture fill the room, with a polished table and an ikon lamp burning softly in the corner. Alyona Ivanovna, small, frail, with sharp features and oily grizzled hair, stands in the center, wrapped in a faded fur cape, scrutinizing a hesitating Raskolnikov. He stands awkwardly near the door, clutching a battered watch, both figures dressed in modest, period-appropriate but worn attire. The air is thick with suspicion and poverty.
ch1-scene4: Raskolnikov stumbles dazedly down the dim, narrow stairwell of a crowded Petersburg tenement, the shadows broken by slivers of fading daylight from a stairwell window. Emerging onto the busy street, he lurches among indifferent passersby, his expression twisted with agitation and self-loathing. The evening is humid and oppressive, with the city's squalor pressed into every cobblestone and facade.
ch1-scene5: A gloomy, low-ceilinged basement tavern cluttered with sticky wooden tables, yellowed from years of smoke. Dim, warm candlelight reflects off dark walls and cheap glassware. Several patrons linger: Raskolnikov sits hunched in the far corner, drinking beer, a reddish flush on his pale face; two drunken men clumsily support each other near the steps; an artisan, slumped in a faded coat, sips beer next to his burly, bearded companion who sleeps fitfully on the bench; a solitary, anxious-looking man in faded civil servant garb occupies another table. The atmosphere is thick with the haze of drink and hardship characteristic of St. Petersburg’s underbelly.
